coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic frameworks join hands to create an anti-cancer nanoplatform based on 808 nm light driving up-conversion nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dan
Xu, Jiating
Yang, Guixin
Zhou, Yuan
Ji, Hongjiao
Bi, Huiting
Gai, Shili
He, Fei
Yang, Piaoping
coredata.dc:description:
Developing multifunctional drug delivery systems for achieving the combination of diagnosis and treatment is highly desirable due to the improved therapeutic effect and minimized side effects. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as emerging porous materials exhibit many intriguing properties for application in biomedicine. Here, MOFs coated up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot liquid-solid-solution (LSS) method for the first time. The encapsulation of UCNPs into MIL-100(Fe) shell leads to core-shell structured UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs. Upon 808 nm laser irradiation, the sample produces abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) of hydroxyl radical (
OH) in the presence of H2O2 derived from Fenton reaction, which are highly toxic to tumor cells. Meanwhile, the MIL-100(Fe) shell efficiently converts the excited laser energy into heat, thus achieving apparent photothermal therapy (PTT) effect. Furthermore, the porous structure and high specific area of the MOFs shell make it possible to load large amount of toxic doxorubicin (DOX). In vivo and in vitro results indicate that DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs markedly suppress the tumor cell growth under very mild 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) based on synergistic (photodynamic, photothermal, and chemo-therapy) effect. This study presents a feasible strategy for developing multifunctional drug delivery system based on MOFs that are responsive to high-penetrated 808 nm laser.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Up-conversion nanoparticles
Photodynamic therapy
MOFs
Synergistic therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of OA-coated NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd (designated as UCNPs)
In vitro cell viability of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
In vitro cytotoxicity of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
Up-conversion luminescence microscopy (UCLM) observation of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
Animal experiments and ex vivo histological staining
Synthesis and characterization of the samples
In vitro and in vivo toxicity study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs.
TEM images of (A) NaGdF4:Yb,Tm, (B) NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd, (C and D) UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs. Insets are their corresponding particle size distributions.

(A) UC emission spectra of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm under 980 nm laser excitation, NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd under 808 nm laser excitation, and UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs under 808 nm laser excitation with the same density of 0.5 W/cm2. Insets are the digital photographs of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm (left) under 980 nm irradiation and NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd (right) particles under 808 nm irradiation. Decay curves at wavelengths of (B) 450, (C) 475 and (D) 650 nm.

(A) In vitro UCL imaging properties: Inverted fluorescence microscope images of HeLa cells incubated with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) for 3 h at 37 °C, scale bars for all images are 25 nm. In vitro and in vivo CT imaging properties: (B) in vitro CT imaging properties. (a) CT images of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with different concentrations; (b) CT values of the aqueous solution of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) versus the different concentrations; CT images of a tumor-bearing mouse: (c, d) pre-injection and (e, f) after injection in situ. (C) In vitro MR imaging properties: (a) T 1-weighted MR images and relaxivity values of the aqueous solutions of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs versus the particle concentrations recorded using a 3 T MR scanner; (b) T 2-weighted MR images and relaxivity values of the aqueous solutions of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs versus the particle concentrations recorded using a 3 T MR scanner.

(A) Cell viability for L929 cells incubated with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with different concentrations for 12 h. Cell viability was measured by MTT assay, data represents mean ± SD (n = 3). (B) Hemolytic assay of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs by human red blood cells.
Drug-release behavior of DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs at pH = 4.4, 5.0 and 7.0 phosphate buffers at 37 °C without and with 808 nm laser irradiation.

(A) The emission spectrum of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd under 808 nm laser excitation (0.5 W/cm2) and the absorption spectrum of MIL-100(Fe) shell. (B) Schematic illustration of the MIL-100(Fe) mediated PDT process. (C) CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated with culture medium (control) and UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs under 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2). All the cells were marked with DCFH-DA. Scale bars for all images are 250 μm.

(A) The infrared thermal images and (B) the corresponding temperature profiles of a tumor-bearing mouse after injection of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs and normal saline (0.1 mL, 500 µg/mL) as a function of the irradiation time irradiated by 808 nm light (0.5 W/cm2).

(A) In vitro cell viabilities of HeLa cells incubated for 24 h with cell-culture, pure DOX, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min, and DOX-UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min at varied concentrations. (B) CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated with cell-culture, pure DOX, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min, and DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min (1 mL, 500 µg/mL). All the cells are marked with calcein AM and PI. Scale bars for all images are 100 μm.

In vivo anti-cancer properties. (A) The body weight and (B) relative tumor volume of tumor bearing mice in different groups versus the treatment time. (C) The weight and (D) representative photographs of tumor excised from tumor-bearing mice. * p < 0.01 versus control group. The data in Fig. 10 (A–C) is average calculated by five mice each group. (E) Representative photographs of tumor-bearing mice treated with normal saline, pure DOX, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min, and DOX-UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min (0.1 mL, 500 µg/mL) on the 14th day.

head.ce:title: Metal-organic frameworks join hands to create an anti-cancer nanoplatform based on 808 nm light driving up-conversion nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Sciences and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up-conversion nanoparticle encapsulated metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) nanoparticles (UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs) were firstly prepared by a one-pot liquid-solid-solution (LSS) method. The DOX loaded sample exhibits excellent tumor inhibition efficiency under mild 808 nm laser irradiation due to the synergistic (photodynamic, photothermal, and chemo-therapy) effect. Owing to the intrinsic up-conversion luminescence, CT and MRI imaging, a multiple imaging guided therapy has been realized.

Developing multifunctional drug delivery systems for achieving the combination of diagnosis and treatment is highly desirable due to the improved therapeutic effect and minimized side effects. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as emerging porous materials exhibit many intriguing properties for application in biomedicine. Here, MOFs coated up-conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot liquid-solid-solution (LSS) method for the first time. The encapsulation of UCNPs into MIL-100(Fe) shell leads to core-shell structured UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs. Upon 808 nm laser irradiation, the sample produces abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) of hydroxyl radical (
OH) in the presence of H2O2 derived from Fenton reaction, which are highly toxic to tumor cells. Meanwhile, the MIL-100(Fe) shell efficiently converts the excited laser energy into heat, thus achieving apparent photothermal therapy (PTT) effect. Furthermore, the porous structure and high specific area of the MOFs shell make it possible to load large amount of toxic doxorubicin (DOX). In vivo and in vitro results indicate that DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs markedly suppress the tumor cell growth under very mild 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2) based on synergistic (photodynamic, photothermal, and chemo-therapy) effect. This study presents a feasible strategy for developing multifunctional drug delivery system based on MOFs that are responsive to high-penetrated 808 nm laser.

ce:section.ce:para: The exploitation of nanomedicine delivery systems in cancer therapy is crucial to meet one of the deadliest diseases (cancer) demand and subsequent side effects issue [1-6]. Among various nanomedicine delivery devices, inorganic organic hybrid materials as drug carriers have become the promising nanomedicine delivery systems due to their high stability, catalytic activity, and biosecurity [7–10]. For perfect inorganic organic hybrid materials performance, the strategies focus primarily on development of new inorganic organic hybrid therapeutic materials and improvement of pre-existing inorganic organic hybrid therapeutic materials, such as enhancing drug loading capacity, increasing delivery systems targeting, biological security, and therapeutic index [11,12].

In contrast, metal–organic frameworks are an attractive kind of hybrid porous network materials composed of single metal nodes or metal clusters which are connected by polytopic organic ligands. It has been extensively developed in heterogeneous catalysis, gas storage, separation, biomedicine and other applications [13-19]. In particular, applications in biomedicine such as carrier, photosensitizer, and photo-thermal therapy (PTT) reagent have made them objects of extensive study [20-22]. MOFs have been regarded as prospective tools to address the challenge of drug delivery due to their beneficial features of tunable size, large surface area, tunable chemical pores, and intrinsic biodegradability [23-29]. Among the many advances in this area, two things are really important in developing the chemistry of MOFs: on one hand, the advantages of MOFs are not solely limited to the features of their pore structures and functions. The morphology and size of MOFs nanocrystals can be precisely controlled according to the reported synthesis process [30]. Recently, several researchers have reviewed the application of MOFs nanocrystals in biomedicine field [31]. For instance, the average pore diameter of MIL-100(Fe)-type MOF is about 3.9 nm and is useful for storage/delivery of hydrophobic drug DHA. Thus, the loading and synchronously delivery of DHA and Fe(III) ions in MIL-100(Fe)-type MOF was successfully used for cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells) therapy [32]. On the other hand, MOFs have been considered as an ideal fourth generation photosensitizer of PDT for cancer, especiallybecauseof the choice of organic linker is the porphyrin derivatives photosensitizers [33,34], while it is also preliminary. Therefore, it is desired to further study the cancer treatment methods of MOFs, and other characteristics and applications need to be further explored [20].

Enlightened by these merits, we report a new case of using Fe3+ doped MOFs nanoparticles (MIL-100(Fe) NPs) for realizing the synergistic treatment of tumors by combining chemotherapy, PTT and PDT. On one hand, the porous MIL-100(Fe) NPs are helpful for loading drug (DOX) molecules, leading to a high anti-cancer efficiency in combination therapy. The resulting particles, once loaded, show no premature leakage and an efficient release. On the other hand, the in vitro experiments confirm that the MIL-100(Fe) NPs catalyze the production of hydroxyl radical (
OH) in the presence of H2O2 under the laser irradiation base on Fenton reaction. As such, the as-developed MIL-100(Fe) NPs not only can greatly improve the conversion of laser energy into heat but also promote ROS generation under laser irradiation. More importantly, the in vivo experimental studies demonstrate that the administration of MIL-100(Fe) NPs significantly inhibits the growth of tumors due to the combination of chemo-therapy, PTT and PDT. This work provides a valuable means to treat tumors by a combination of multimode therapy based on MIL-100(Fe) NPs stimulated by 808 nm laser irradiation.

All chemicals and reagents were used as received in this experiment without any further purification. Gd2O3, Yb2O3, Tm2O3, and Nd2O3 (99.99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., shanghai, China. DOX, NaF, CF3COONa, benzyl ether, oleylamine, Fe(acac)3, triethylamine (TEA), 1, 3, 5-trimesic acid (H3BTC), cyclohexane, oleic acid (OA, 90%), 1-octadecene (ODE, 90%), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), 3–4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 2, 7-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), 4, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), calcein AM and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of OA-coated NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd (designated as UCNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, mono-dispersed NaGdF4:Yb,Tm nanoparticles were obtained by the following procedure: 1.0 mmol of RE (OA)3 (RE = 80% Gd + 19.5% Yb + 0.5% Tm) and 0.21 g of NaF were charged into a 100 mL three-necked flask containing 30 mL of a mixed solution of OA and ODE (V OA/V ODE = 1/1), and then gradually heated up to 110 °C under vacuum. The temperature was maintained for 30 min until no bubbles were formed to remove oxygen and moisture. After that, the mixture was heated to 310 °C under N2 protection and held for 1.5 h to yield a pale yellow homogeneous solution. After the reaction, the system was allowed to cool to room temperature. After NaGdF4:Yb,Tm NPs were collected by centrifugation and washed alternately with cyclohexane and ethanol, 5 mL of cyclohexane was used to disperse the resulting nanoparticles for later use.

For the synthesis of the core-shell, 0.5 mmol of RE(CF3COO)3 (RE = 60% Gd + 10% Yb + 30% Nd), 0.068 g of CF3COONa and the nanoparticles obtained in the previous step were injected into a 100 mL flask containing 30 mL of the mixed solution of OA and ODE (V OA /V ODE = 1/1). The obtained mixed solution was heated to 120 °C under vacuum, and maintained the temperature for more than half an hour until no bubbles were formed. Then, the temperature of solution was raised to 320 °C in an N2 atmosphere for 1 h. At this point, a dark yellow solution should be obtained and allowed to cool to room temperature. Methods for collection and storage are the same as NaGdF4:Yb,Tm NPs. The core–shell upconverting nanoparticles NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd are denoted as UCNPs.

At room temperature, 10 mL of benzyl ether, 10 mL of oleylamine and 2 mmol of Fe(acac)3 (acac = acetylacetonate) were added to the round bottom flask and mixed. The solution was heated with stirring to 120 °C under nitrogen protect and this experimental environment was maintained for 2 h. The temperature was then raised to 300 °C and maintained for 1 h. Finally, the system was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The method of collecting and storing the OA-coated Fe3O4 nanoparticles was the same as NaGdF4:Yb,Tm NPs for further use.

The sample was prepared based on previous report with a little change [66]. Briefly, 5 mmol of OA-coated UCNPs and 0.09 g OA-coated Fe3O4 NPs were dispersed into mixture solution (I) consisting of OA (2 mL) and DMSO (4 mL), and 24 mL of H3BTC (0.12 g) were added into mixture solution (II) containing DMSO and H2O (V DMSO/V H2O = 1/5). Then, (II) solution was added into (I) solution with stirring for about 2 h in air. During stirring, 0.2 mL of TEA was injected. After that, the last mixture solution was placed into and kept 120 °C heating for 10 h. The product was first collected by centrifugation, and then washed with ethanol. The precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) nanoparticles.

Firstly, 0.5 g of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) was evenly dispersed in a beaker containing 5 mL of PBS (pH = 7) (the beaker is protected from light). 0.0025 g of DOX was added into the beaker at room temperature. After 24 h of continuous stirring, the mixed solution was centrifuged to remove unloaded drug molecules in the supernatant solution and the precipitate was DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) nanoparticles obtained. Next, an ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer was used to detect the supernatant absorbance of supernatant solution at a wavelength of 480 nm. According to the standard curve of DOX A 480 nm = 0.0187C + 0.0233, the loading efficiency and slow release efficiency of DOX in nanoparticles can be calculated by the following formula: IE% =
(
M
1
-
M
2
)
M
1
× 100 %
(M1 is the total drug, M2 is the drug in the supernatant), achieving a loading efficiency of 72%, which is close to loading efficiency (73.6%) of pure MIL-100(Fe).

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were recorded on a Rigaku D/max-TTR-III diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out a FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin with a field emission gun operating at 200 kV. N2 absorption/desorption isotherms were obtained at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP TriStar II instrument. The specific surface area was determined through the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The pore size distribution was calculated by the Barret-Joner-Halenda (BJH) method. UCL emission spectra were obtained on Edinburgh FLS 980 using 808 nm laser diode controller (ADR-1860) and 980 nm laser product (MW-GX-980/2000mW) as separate laser sources. All the UC spectra in our experiment were gained under the same condition with the same signal amplification. UV–Vis spectra were detected by UV-1601 spectrophotometer. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were recorded using Leica SP8. The UCL images were obtained on Nikon Ti-S with an external 808 nm laser irradiation. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) experiments were performed on a 3.0 Tesla (T) whole-body MR scanner (Achieva TX, Philips Medical System, Netherlands) at room temperature.

NIR laser-induced in vitro PDT performance of materials on HeLa cancer cells (human cervical cancer cell lines) was observed by CLSM. First, HeLa cells (∼5 × 104/well) were placed in a 6-well culture plate with a slide and cultured in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C to form a monolayer, and 2 mL/well test material (1 mg/mL) was added. After a further 4 h of incubation, the residual material was thoroughly removed by washing three times with PBS. At this point, cells that had swallowed the test materials were irradiated intermittently with an 808 nm laser (0.5 W/m2) for 5 min. Finally, cells were stained with DCFH-DA for 20 min and recorded by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The MTT assay was used to assess the biocompatibility of UCNPs@ MIL-100(Fe) NPs to L929 fibroblasts. First, L929 fibroblasts were seeded in 96-well plates (∼7 × 103/well) and cultured overnight. After forming a monolayer, 0.1 mL of a half-fold dilution of UCNP @ MIL-100 (Fe) NPs (8, 16, 32, 63, 125, 250 and 500 μg/mL) was added to each well. The 96-well plate is left for 12 h so that the material is phagocytosed by the cells. Next, 0.02 mL of MTT stock solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h (5% CO2, 37 °C). After thoroughly removing the suspension, 0.15 mL of DMSO solution was added to each well. Based on previous findings, live cells through the metabolism can be used to reduce MTT with cytochrome c for producing formazan which is soluble in DMSO solution, suggesting that the number of viable cells is proportional to the absorbance of formazan at 488 nm. Microplate reader can be used to read the absorbance of formazan at 488 nm. The data in this test is averaged over three parallel groups for each concentration.

Red blood cells (RBCs) are typically used for hemolysis assay, so EDTA.K2 stabilized human blood is firstly washed with PBS and centrifuged until the supernatant becomes clear to obtain red blood cells. The resulting RBCs were then diluted by PBS with the volume proportion of 1:10. 1.2 mL of PBS, deionized water and different concentrations of UCNPs@MIL-100 (Fe) (8, 16, 32, 63, 125 and 250 μg/mL) were separately placed in a 2 mL tube with 0.3 mL of diluted RBC and mixed. After the mixed solution was allowed to stand for 2 h, a UV–vis spectrometer was used to detect the absorbance in the upper supernatant at 541 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the principle of the MTT assay, we still enabled it to assess the cytotoxicity of DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) complexes on cancer cells. The detection process is similar to the biocompatibility of L929 fibroblasts in vitro. Cervical cancer cells were used as an example of cancer cells in this experiment. HeLa cancer cells were cultivated with various certain concentrations of pure DOX, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation and DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation in the MTT assay. Besides, the cell treated with culture medium was employed as control blank group.

The detection process of dye test in vitro is very similar to the photodynamic test with the only difference being that live and dead cells were stained with calcein (AM, 2 mM) and propidium iodide (PI, 5 mM) for 1 h instead of DCFH-DA stained for 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up-conversion luminescence microscopy (UCLM) observation of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, HeLa cells (∼5 × 104/well) were seeded into 6-well plates with glass slides and cultured overnight to form a monolayer. Then, 2 mL of prepared UCNPs@ MIL-100(Fe) (0.5 mg/mL) was then incubated for a designated period of time (3 h) under simulated normal body temperature. To remove material that is not phagocytosed by the cells, the cells are washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, 1 mL of formaldehyde (2.5%) was added to each well, the cells were fixed for 10 min and rinsed three more times with PBS. Finally, an up-conversion luminescence microscope (UCLM) instrument equipped with an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Ti-S) and an external CW 980 nm laser diode was used to monitor all collected cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animal experiments and ex vivo histological staining
ce:section.ce:para: Each female Kunming mouse (about 25 g) was implanted with 100 µL of U14 cells (murine cervical cancer cell line) on the left armpit and divided into 4 groups. When the tumor grows to the size of the soybean, tumor-bearing mice were challenged with various test conditions including normal saline, pure DOX, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) nanoparticles containing 808 nm laser irradiation, and DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with 808 nm laser irradiation. In consistence with in vitro experiment, the dosage of DOX injected in mice was normalized to be equivalent to the theoretical loading amount of DOX in DOX-UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe). So, the dosage of free DOX injected in mice was 3.6 mg/mL. The group treated with normal saline was used as a control black group. After intravenous injection of different test materials (1 mg/mL) for 1 h, the tumor site of the irradiated mice was exposed to 808 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min. In this work, each mouse had been treated for two days and the tumor volume and body weight of each mouse were recorded at the same time. After 14 days of treatment, the tumor tissues and typical organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) of the mice were dissected for further histological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: Here, water soluble-stabilized UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs were prepared by LSS method for the first time. As shown in Fig. 1 , the coordination reaction of metal oleate (solid) and ligand were mainly performed at the interfaces of DMSO-OA liquid phase (liquid) and water-DMSO solutions (solution). The formation mechanism of water soluble-stabilized UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs could be proposed as follows. First, OA was added to dissolve and stabilize the iron (III) oleate cluster and oleate coated UCNPs. When DMSO solution containing H3BTC ligand was added, an oil/water (O/W) interface was formed, and the UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs were generated gradually at the interface by an anion exchange process when the temperature was increase to 120 °C. TEA was chosen as a structure-directing agent (SDA) to regulate the crystal growth of MIL-100(Fe) NPs in this study.

The morphology of as-grown NaGdF4:Yb,Tm, NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd, and NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd@MIL-100(Fe) (UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe)) was examined by TEM. The uniform shape and good monodispersity of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm (Fig. 2 A) with mean size of 22 nm and NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd (Fig. 2B) with a size around 25 nm are showed in Fig. 2. After coating MIL-100(Fe) shell, the particle size of UCNPs@ MIL-100(Fe) NPs increases to about 32 nm (Fig. 2C and 2D). As indicated by the circles and arrows, an apparent core–shell structure with a layer of 3–5 nm has been realized, indicating the formation of MOFs shell functioning as drug containers. The presence of Na, Gd, Yb, Tm, Nd, and Fe elements can be provided by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. S1). In addition, the BET surface area of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) was determined to be about 109 m2/g (Fig. S2). Compared with BET surface area (120 m2/g) of pure MIL-100(Fe), the surface area (109 m2/g) of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) is slightly decreased, however, the large surface area and porous structure imply this has no obvious influence on the good biomedical performance of the particle compare to the loading efficiency of drug. The results indicate that the MIL-100(Fe) shell can be served as an efficient nanocarrier for drug loading and delivery. The crystallization nature of as-grown NaGdF4:Yb,Tm, NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd, and UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) were examined by XRD (Fig. S3). The characteristic peak of MIL-100 (Fe) was obtained in the diffraction pattern of the composite, and all peaks in the patterns can be well assigned to the reflections of NaGdF4 hexagonal phase (JCPDS Card No. 27-0699).

Fig. 3 presents the up-converting emission spectra and corresponding luminescent photographs of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm, NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd, and UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs examined with the excitation of 808 and 980 nm diode lasers with the same power density (0.5 W/cm2), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3A, all the materials give similar spectral distributions and pronounced blue (450 nm: 1D2 → 3F4, 475 nm: 1G4 → 3H6) and red (650 nm: 3F2 → 3H6) emissions assigned to the transitions of Tm3+ ions. Besides, the decrease of intensity and the change of red-blue emission ratio of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) compared with NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd illustrate the excellent energy conversion process. the decay curves of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm and UCNPs under 808 or 980 nm laser excitation are showed in Fig. 3B-D. The lifetimes of the two samples increase at 450 nm (1D2 → 3F4 transition), 475 nm (1G4 → 3H6 transition), and 650 nm (3F2 → 3H6 transition), further proving the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [67-69].

In Fig. 3A, the blue light emission is still be detected in UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) under 808 nm laser irradiation, indicating that it is possible to be used for UCL imaging to diagnose tumor location or track the carrier position. As shown in Fig. 4 A, after the cells were incubated with the complex for three hours and irradiated with 808 nm laser for 5 min with a density of 0.5 W/cm2, a significant intracellular UCL signal was detected in the cells. Meanwhile, the complex can also be used as an X-ray CT imaging contrast agent due to Gd and Yb of high atomic number elements doped crystals with strong X-ray attenuation. The feasibility of the composite in vitro and in vivo for CT imaging was evaluated by phantom images taken at different composite concentrations (Fig. 4B). The CT signal intensity of UCNP@MIL-100(Fe) NPs in aqueous solution continuously increases with increasing concentration. The CT value (Hounsfield units, HU) was plotted as monotonic linear-increase with the UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) concentration. The X-ray absorption coefficient of the composite was calculated to be about 1098.6 HU, which shows a high positive contrast enhancement. The X-ray attenuation capability of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs in vivo was also tested to further assess its performance as a CT imaging agent in vivo. As demonstrated by the three-dimensional (3D) CT images in Fig. 4B(c–f)), the mean CT value in the tumor site of the mice is up to 351.8 HU which is much higher than CT value before injection of 52.2 HU. The CT images were taken from tumor-bearing mice treated by the transversal position with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs (0.5 mg/mL, 100 µL) at 1 h. It can be, therefore, concluded that as-synthesized UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs can be served as efficient contrast agent for CT imaging based on the above in vitro and in vivo CT results. In addition, the complex can be used for T 1 /T 2 MRI agents due to the presence of Gd and Fe elements [70,71]. Longitudinal and transverse relaxivity values at each concentration were measured and summarized in Fig. 4C, showing that the reciprocal values of relaxation times were plotted as a linear-increase with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) concentration. The longitudinal and transverse relaxivity values were calculated to be r 1 = 0.0107 mg−1/ms and r 2 = 0.0226 mg−1/ms, respectively. In general, r 2/r 1 ratio is an important parameter to determine whether a contrast agent is T 1 or T 2 predominant in MRI. The high r 2/r 1 ratio (>8) indicates the contrast dominated by T 2, while the lower ratio (<5) is dominated by T 1. Obviously, the UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) complex (r 2/r 1 = 2.112) is the dominant T 1 imaging MRI agent. T 1 and T 2-weighted MR images of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs show sustained signal enhancement with increasing sample contents in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo toxicity study
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that the biocompatibility of materials is a critical reference for potential biomedical applications. The biocompatibility was assessed by cell viability for L929 cells to determine using MTT assay. The cells were incubated with different concentrations of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs (8, 16, 32, 63, 125, 250 and 500 µg/mL, respectively) for 12 h. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs exhibit good biocompatibility in vitro. No appreciable negative effect on the normal cells even up to the highest concentration of 500 µg/mL can be found. Besides, good biocompatibility with red blood cells also is essential condition of ideal drug carrier for biological applications. Hemolytic assay was further to confirm the biosecurity of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs using for intravenous injection in vivo. Hemolytic results and digital photography were shown in Fig. 5B. In contrast, no appreciable red colour is shown in the experiments group containing UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs with different concentrations (from 8 to 250 µg/mL). Meanwhile, UV–vis absorbance values (541 nm) of less than 5% were obtained in all the experiments group. Trace hemolytic results demonstrate that UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs scarcely damage erythrocytes and have no side effects on red blood cells. As mentioned above, the biocompatibility and biosecurity of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs are very excellent.

UV–vis spectra were used to assess the stability of DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs. As shown, both the UV–vis spectra and the images demonstrate that no difference and aggregation is detected even though DOX loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs incubated in fetal bovine serum (FBS, pH = 7, Fig. S4A) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7, Fig. S4B) for up to 7 days. It is means that the high stability of the DOX loading system.

Next, the drug release behavior of the UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs was further examined in PBS at various pH (pH = 4.4, 5.0, and 7.0), as shown in Fig. 6 . Drug release performance is pH-dependent and increased in acidic environments due to the increased protonation of the amino group in DOX under acidic condition, which is evaluated by UV–vis spectrum at 490 nm. Blood is neutral and intracellular tumor sites are the acidic microenvironment (pH = 4.5–6.0), so preferential release of DOX in an acidic environment during intravenous injection minimizes the risk of adverse reactions to normal cells. Meanwhile, this mechanism may benefit the on-demand chemotherapy, which is attributed to the cumulative action at tumor sites through the enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR effect). The drug release process irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser was measured again (0.5 W/cm2). The results show that drug release efficiency significantly improved because of photothermal effect. The drug release efficiency is up to 79.8%, 58.9%, and 30.2% under laser irradiation at the pH values of 4.0, 5.5 and 7.0, respectively. The synergistic therapy and drug release at elevated temperature are beneficial to therapeutic effect (Fig. 6).

Fig. 7 A shows that NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd (UCNPs) convert low-energy NIR light (808 nm) to higher-energy UV–vis light which could be absorpted by MIL-100(Fe) shell, and then the proposed mechanism of the MIL-100(Fe) mediated photodynamic therapy process is revealed (Fig. 7B). After light stimulation, electrons are transferred from O2– to Fe3+ to form the reduced Fe2+ ion during this course because charge carriers are generated in Fe-O clusters. Based on Fenton-like reaction, the transport photon energy can further react with H2O2 to yield the
OH radical, which can attack at the cancer cells via photodynamic therapy process. This conceive has been proved by means of intracellular photodynamic effect test. In this test, DCFH-DA dye with non-fluorescent and low concentration is assigned as ROS-index probe, and it can be hydrolyzed into water soluble DCFH by esterase in cells. Strong fluorescence DCF (λ ex = 488 nm) is obtained via a oxidation reaction of ROS with DCFH and can be detected at a wavelength of 525 nm. So, bright green fluorescence of DCF is a characteristic of the presence of ROS. Based on the above theories, strong green fluorescence is recorded within the cells incubated with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs exposed under 808 nm irradiation for 5 min at a density of demonstrates, while the HeLa cells incubated with culture medium hardly show perceive fluorescence, illustrating that UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs have intracellular photodynamic effect (Fig. 7C).

Another focus of this study is to explore UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs as PTT agent. Firstly, UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs as PTT agent was realized based on MIL-100(Fe) shell, and the light absorption of MIL-100(Fe) NPs has been confirmed. The photothermal conversion capability of UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs in vivo was evaluated by recording the temperature rise upon irradiation with 808 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2) for 5 min. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the temperature rise in the tumor site of mouse treated with UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs follows a time-dependent manner. The temperature in the therapeutic area reveals high contrast infrared thermal images and quickly increase to about 57 °C (Fig. 8A and B), which reaches cell death temperature (40–60 °C). In contrast, for the group treated with normal saline, temperature barely changes upon irradiation. So, the experiment illustrates that UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs can be served as an excellent candidate for PTT application.

To examine the synergistic therapeutic effect in vitro under external stimulus by 808 nm laser irradiation, the cell viability of HeLa cells was examined after incubation with DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe), UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs and free DOX for 12 h and then under 808 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 5 min irradiation). The concentrations of the materials used in treating HeLa cancer cells are 500, 250, 125, 63, 32, 16, and 8 μg/mL, respectively. The concentrations of DOX used in treating HeLa cancer cells were normalized to be the equivalent to the theoretical loading amount of DOX in DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs, and values are 1.8, 0.9, 0.45, 0.23, 0.11, 0.056, and 0.028 mg/mL, respectively. The MTT method was assigned to measure the cytotoxicity of different therapeutic systems. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the combination of DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs and laser irradiation has a much higher potency to destroy HeLa cells at all the tested concentrations than either UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs or chemotherapy (free DOX). HeLa cells incubated with DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs induce an inhibition rate as high as 78.3%. The largely improved therapeutic effect can be attributed to the synergistic therapy and drug release at elevated temperature. Then, CLSM was used to collect HeLa cells treated with different conditions and labelled with calcein AM and PI where AM made the dead cells red and PI made the live cells green. Fig. 9B shows that few cells survive for cancer cells incubated with DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs under NIR irradiation, which is very similar to MTT results.

Encouraged by the inhibiting tumor effectiveness of the DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs in vitro, the contrast experiments in vivo were also studied, and the results are given in Fig. 10 . A steady growth trend of the body weight of the mice was presented during the experimental period, indicates no obvious systemic toxicity upon different treatments (Fig. 10A). So, the maintained body weight also indicates that it agrees to the experiment result in vitro. Moreover, the relative tumor volumes (V/V 0) are then plotted as a function of treated time, as shown in Fig. 10B. For tumor-bearing mice treated only by pure DOX, the tumor growth inhibition efficacy is much lower than that of dual mode and tri-mode therapy (UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs or DOX-loaded UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs upon 808 nm NIR laser stimulation at a density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 5 min. To more clearly explain the potential toxicity in vivo, the photographs and weights of the tumor tissues corresponding with mice in all treatment groups are given in Fig. 10C and D, which directly illustrate the high inhibition effectiveness of the materials. In addition, since considerable localization of the NPs in the mononuclear phagocyte systems such as liver and spleen is revealed by histology analysis of the major organs such as heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), which were performed 14 days after treatments to evaluate the potential toxicity effects on these major organs. As shown in Fig. S5, no evident organ damage or inflammatory lesion is found in all major organs, further confirming no/very low toxicity of the NPs in vivo at our tested dose.

In summary, the encapsulation of NaGdF4:Yb,Tm@NaGdF4:Yb,Nd into the MIL-100(Fe) shell was easily realized by a one-pot LSS method for the first time. The obtained UCNPs@MIL-100(Fe) NPs not only promote abundant ROS generation in the presence of H2O2 under the 808 nm laser irradiation based on Fenton reaction, but also efficiently improve the conversion of laser energy into heat. Moreover, porous MIL-100(Fe) NPs enhance the loading of DOX to achieve the synergistic effect of chemo-therapy, PTT and PDT. The treatment mode guided by multimode imaging greatly suppress the tumor cell growth in vivo and in vitro under mild 808 nm irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51472058, 51772059, 51702070 and 51602072), the Special Innovation Talents of Harbin Science and Technology (2016RAXXJ005), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M581430, 2016T90269), Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund (LBH-Z14052, LBH-TZ0607), the PHD Student Research and Innovation Fund of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and the Fundamental Research funds for the Central Universities are greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.03.101.
